source_paper_title: On Averaging and Extrapolation for Gradient Descent
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Luner_2025, title={On Averaging and Extrapolation for Gradient Descent}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2024.0449}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2024.0449}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Luner, Alan and Grimmer, Benjamin}, year={2025}, month=july }
context:
Section 3.2.1 (Discussion of Conditions (C1) and (C2)), page 12.

That is, numerical results suggest that the converses of Theorem 3.1 and Theorem 3.2 are true, i.e., (C1)/(C2) hold if and only if averaging is not beneficial. We state this formally in the following conjecture.

Conjecture 3.3. Consider gradient descent with stepsizes \(h = (h_0, \dots, h_{N-1}) > 0\). For any \(L, D > 0\), if the worst-case final iterate objective gap is not characterized by (C1), then there exists nondegenerate \(\sigma\) such that
\[\max_{(x_0,f)\in \mathcal{F}_{L,D}} f\Bigl(\sum_{j=0}^N \sigma_j x_j\Bigr) - \inf f < \frac{LD^2}{4\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i + 2}.\]
Similarly, if the worst-case final iterate gradient norm is not characterized by (C2), then there exists nondegenerate \(\sigma\) such that
\[\max_{(x_0,f)\in \mathcal{F}_{L,D}} \|\nabla f\bigl(\sum_{j=0}^N \sigma_j x_j\bigr)\| < \frac{LD}{\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i + 1}.\]
title: Prove tight worst-case characterization of gradient descent averaging benefit conditions
background_latex:
Let \(f:\mathbb{R}^m\to\mathbb{R}\) be convex with \(L\)-Lipschitz continuous gradient (\(L\)-smooth). Let \(x^\star\in\arg\min f\) exist, and assume an initial point \(x_0\) satisfies \(\|x_0-x^\star\|\le D\). Consider (unconstrained) gradient descent with a fixed, predetermined stepsize sequence \(h=(h_0,\dots,h_{N-1})\) with \(h_k>0\):
\[
 x_{k+1}=x_k-\frac{h_k}{L}\nabla f(x_k),\qquad k=0,\dots,N-1.
\]
(So the iterates \(x_0,\dots,x_N\) are generated by this recursion.)

An averaging scheme is a weight vector \(\sigma=(\sigma_0,\dots,\sigma_N)\in\Delta_{N+1}\), where the simplex is
\[
\Delta_{N+1}:=\{\sigma\in\mathbb{R}^{N+1}_+ : \sum_{j=0}^N \sigma_j=1\}.
\]
The reported point under averaging is
\[
 x_\sigma:=\sum_{j=0}^N \sigma_j x_j.
\]
The degenerate averaging scheme that returns the last iterate is \(\sigma^{\mathrm{last}}:=(0,\dots,0,1)\), for which \(x_{\sigma^{\mathrm{last}}}=x_N\). An averaging scheme is called nondegenerate if \(x_\sigma\ne x_N\) (equivalently, \(\sigma\ne\sigma^{\mathrm{last}}\)).

Define the worst-case last-iterate bounds
\[
\text{(C1)}\quad \max_{(x_0,f):\ \|x_0-x^\star\|\le D} \bigl(f(x_N)-f(x^\star)\bigr) = \frac{LD^2}{4\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i + 2},
\]
\[
\text{(C2)}\quad \max_{(x_0,f):\ \|x_0-x^\star\|\le D} \|\nabla f(x_N)\| = \frac{LD}{\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i + 1}.
\]
(These equalities assert that the displayed expressions exactly characterize the worst-case objective gap and worst-case gradient norm at the last iterate for the given stepsizes.)

The paper proves one direction: if (C1) holds, then \(\sigma^{\mathrm{last}}\) uniquely minimizes the worst-case objective gap over all \(\sigma\in\Delta_{N+1}\); similarly, if (C2) holds, then \(\sigma^{\mathrm{last}}\) uniquely minimizes the worst-case gradient norm over all \(\sigma\in\Delta_{N+1}\). It then proposes a converse statement as a conjecture.
problem_definition_latex:
Establish (or refute) the following converse characterizations for gradient descent with fixed stepsizes \(h=(h_0,\dots,h_{N-1})>0\):

1) Objective-gap converse: If the worst-case last-iterate objective gap is not characterized by (C1), i.e.,
\[
\max_{(x_0,f):\ \|x_0-x^\star\|\le D} \bigl(f(x_N)-f(x^\star)\bigr) \ne \frac{LD^2}{4\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i + 2},
\]
then there exists a nondegenerate averaging scheme \(\sigma\in\Delta_{N+1}\) such that
\[
\max_{(x_0,f):\ \|x_0-x^\star\|\le D} \bigl(f(x_\sigma)-f(x^\star)\bigr) 
< \frac{LD^2}{4\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i + 2}.
\]

2) Gradient-norm converse: If the worst-case last-iterate gradient norm is not characterized by (C2), i.e.,
\[
\max_{(x_0,f):\ \|x_0-x^\star\|\le D} \|\nabla f(x_N)\| \ne \frac{LD}{\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i + 1},
\]
then there exists a nondegenerate averaging scheme \(\sigma\in\Delta_{N+1}\) such that
\[
\max_{(x_0,f):\ \|x_0-x^\star\|\le D} \|\nabla f(x_\sigma)\| 
< \frac{LD}{\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i + 1}.
\]

Equivalently, prove that (C1) holds if and only if averaging cannot improve the worst-case objective-gap guarantee over returning \(x_N\), and (C2) holds if and only if averaging cannot improve the worst-case gradient-norm guarantee over returning \(x_N\).

\[

It then proposes a converse statement as a conjecture.

Establish (or refute) the following converse characterizations for gradient descent with fixed stepsizes \(h=(h_0,\dots,h_{N-1})>0\):

1) Objective-gap converse: If the worst-case last-iterate objective gap is not characterized by (C1), i.e.,
\[
\max_{(x_0,f):\ \|x_0-x^\star\|\le D} \bigl(f(x_N)-f(x^\star)\bigr) \ne \frac{LD^2}{4\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i + 2},
\]
then there exists a nondegenerate averaging scheme \(\sigma\in\Delta_{N+1}\) such that
\[
\max_{(x_0,f):\ \|x_0-x^\star\|\le D} \bigl(f(x_\sigma)-f(x^\star)\bigr) 
< \frac{LD^2}{4\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i + 2}.
\]

2) Gradient-norm converse: If the worst-case last-iterate gradient norm is not characterized by (C2), i.e.,
\[
\max_{(x_0,f):\ \|x_0-x^\star\|\le D} \|\nabla f(x_N)\| \ne \frac{LD}{\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i + 1},
\]
then there exists a nondegenerate averaging scheme \(\sigma\in\Delta_{N+1}\) such that
\[
\max_{(x_0,f):\ \|x_0-x^\star\|\le D} \|\nabla f(x_\sigma)\| 
< \frac{LD}{\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i + 1}.
\]

Equivalently, prove that (C1) holds if and only if averaging cannot improve the worst-case objective-gap guarantee over returning \(x_N\), and (C2) holds if and only if averaging cannot improve the worst-case gradient-norm guarantee over returning \(x_N\).
open_problem_quote:
That is, numerical results suggest that the converses of Theorem 3.1 and Theorem 3.2 are true, i.e., (C1)/(C2) hold if and only if averaging is not beneficial. We state this formally in the following conjecture.

Conjecture 3.3. Consider gradient descent with stepsizes \(h = (h_0, \dots, h_{N-1}) > 0\). For any \(L, D > 0\), if the worst-case final iterate objective gap is not characterized by (C1), then there exists nondegenerate \(\sigma\) such that
\[\max_{(x_0,f)\in \mathcal{F}_{L,D}} f\Bigl(\sum_{j=0}^N \sigma_j x_j\Bigr) - \inf f < \frac{LD^2}{4\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i + 2}.\]
Similarly, if the worst-case final iterate gradient norm is not characterized by (C2), then there exists nondegenerate \(\sigma\) such that
\[\max_{(x_0,f)\in \mathcal{F}_{L,D}} \|\nabla f\bigl(\sum_{j=0}^N \sigma_j x_j\bigr)\| < \frac{LD}{\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i + 1}.\]
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The conjecture can be tested for fixed small \(N\) and stepsize sequences by solving the associated PEP semidefinite programs for both the last iterate and the best averaged iterate, and checking whether an improving nondegenerate \(\sigma\) exists whenever (C1)/(C2) fail. This makes automated search over stepsize patterns and averaging weights feasible.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate objects (stepsize sequences \(h\) and averaging weights \(\sigma\)) admit a direct computational fitness signal via PEP SDPs approximating the worst-case gaps/gradient norms. An evolutionary loop could search for counterexamples (instances where (C1)/(C2) fail but averaging still does not help, or vice versa) or discover structural patterns suggesting a proof strategy.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
